# ADR-0001: Back rate-limit throttling state with the cluster cache primitive

**ID**: `cpt-api-gateway-adr-distributed-throttling-state`

## Table of Contents

<!-- generated by `cfs toc` -->

## Context and Problem Statement

The API Gateway's rate-limit throttling keeps its state in-process: each zone
owns a `governor` keyed token bucket stored in a `DashMap`
(`gears/system/api-gateway/src/middleware/throttling.rs`). When the gateway runs
as more than one replica, each replica enforces the configured budget
independently, so an operator who configures "N requests/second" for a client
actually permits `N × replica_count`. How should throttling share limit state
across replicas so a configured budget is enforced for the deployment as a
whole, using the platform's existing cluster primitives rather than a new
bespoke datastore?

## Decision Drivers

* **Cross-replica correctness** — a configured rate must bound the whole
  deployment, not one replica.
* **Reuse existing platform primitives** — the workspace already ships a cluster
  cache primitive with version-based compare-and-swap (`ClusterCacheV1` /
  `ClusterCacheBackend` in `cf-cluster-sdk`); a distributed limiter should build
  on it rather than introduce a new dependency (Redis client, etc.).
* **Hot-path latency & runtime safety** — throttling runs on every request in
  async Axum middleware; the design must not block runtime workers or risk
  deadlock on a current-thread runtime.
* **Availability** — the throttler must not turn a cache outage into a
  full-traffic outage unless an operator explicitly opts into that.
* **Backward compatibility** — existing single-replica / in-process behavior
  must remain the default and unchanged for gears that do not opt in.
* **Bounded memory** — per-key state must be reclaimed cluster-wide without an
  ever-growing key set (today handled by a local background pruner).

## Considered Options

* **Option A — Async distributed state store over the cluster cache** (new async
  abstraction; GCRA algorithm reused, cache CAS as the atomic replace).
* **Option B — Literal `governor::state::StateStore` implementation over the
  cluster cache** (bridge the trait's synchronous `measure_and_replace` to the
  async cache with a blocking call).
* **Option C — Local buckets with periodic asynchronous reconciliation to the
  cache** (eventually-consistent write-behind of per-replica counters).
* **Option D — Do nothing** (keep in-process state; document the
  per-replica-multiplication caveat).

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: **Option A — Async distributed state store over the cluster
cache**, because it is the only option that achieves cross-replica correctness
while respecting the async hot path and reusing the existing cluster cache
primitive. The rate-limit state governor stores per key is a single value (the
GCRA theoretical-arrival-time), which maps one-to-one onto a versioned cache
entry, and the cache's version-based compare-and-swap is exactly the atomic
"replace only if unchanged" operation the GCRA update needs — so the cache
becomes the distributed analogue of governor's in-memory replace, just async.

Option B is rejected because `governor`'s `StateStore::measure_and_replace` is
synchronous and owns the retry loop internally; bridging it to an async cache
requires blocking a runtime worker per throttled request and can deadlock on a
current-thread runtime. Option C is rejected as a default because it does not
enforce a hard cross-replica limit (it only approximates one) and adds
reconciliation complexity; it remains a possible future relaxation for very hot
keys. Option D does not solve the problem.

Scope: this decision covers the **rate-limit** (token-bucket / GCRA) throttling
path only. The **in-flight concurrency** limiter (per-key semaphores) is a
different distributed problem (a live lease released on request completion,
closer to a distributed lock) and is explicitly out of scope here; it remains
in-process and may be addressed in a later ADR.

### Consequences

* The gateway gains an internal backend abstraction for rate-limit state with
  two implementations: the existing in-process `governor` path (default) and a
  new cluster-cache-backed path. A zone selects its backend via configuration;
  absence of configuration keeps today's in-process behavior.
* The gateway must resolve `ClusterCacheV1` from the ClientHub during
  initialization (the same mechanism already used to resolve the AuthN resolver
  client) and declare a dependency on the cluster capability. Deployments that
  do not provide a cluster cache can only use in-process zones.
* Per-key state must be serialized to and from cache bytes as the GCRA arrival
  time, and each write must carry a TTL equal to the bucket's full replenish
  window so idle keys self-expire cluster-wide. This replaces the local pruner's
  role for distributed zones and provides the `max_keys` bound implicitly.
* The distributed path must compute arrival times against a **shared wall-clock**
  reference (not per-node monotonic clocks), accepting a bounded error under
  clock skew that must be documented.
* Correctness requires a **linearizable** cache; the design must reject an
  eventually-consistent backend by default and offer an explicit opt-in that
  emits a warning (mirroring the cluster gear's `CasBasedDistributedLockBackend`
  consistency guard).
* Cache unavailability handling must be an explicit per-zone policy. Default is
  **fail-open** (serve the request, emit a warning and a metric) so the cache is
  not a single point of failure for all traffic; operators may opt into
  fail-closed.
* Observability must distinguish enforced rejections from cache-error /
  fail-open events (extend the existing `throttling.rejections` counter with a
  reason/label), so operators can see when distributed enforcement degrades.
* Each throttled request on a distributed zone incurs (amortized) one cache
  round-trip plus bounded CAS retries under contention; this is acceptable for
  identity-keyed budgets and must be load-tested for hot pre-auth IP zones.

### Confirmation

* Design and code review confirming: default (unconfigured) zones remain
  in-process; distributed zones perform no blocking calls on the async hot path.
* Integration test with a linearizable in-memory cache fixture (the cluster
  conformance fixture) asserting that two gateway instances sharing one cache
  enforce a single combined budget, and that CAS conflicts are retried.
* Test asserting fail-open vs fail-closed behavior on injected cache errors, and
  that the corresponding metric/label is emitted.
* Test asserting a distributed zone rejects a non-linearizable cache unless the
  explicit weak-consistency opt-in is set.

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Option A — Async distributed state store over the cluster cache

An async backend abstraction inside the gateway; the distributed implementation
reads the current arrival time via `get`, computes the GCRA decision, and commits
with `compare_and_swap(expected_version, …, ttl)`, retrying a bounded number of
times on `CasConflict`. The in-process `governor` path is kept for local zones.

* Good, because it enforces a true cross-replica limit via atomic CAS.
* Good, because it fits the already-async middleware with no blocking bridge.
* Good, because it reuses the existing cluster cache primitive and its native
  TTL and versioning (no new external dependency).
* Good, because per-key state auto-expires cluster-wide via TTL, bounding memory.
* Neutral, because the GCRA update (~a dozen lines) is reimplemented since
  `governor`'s algorithm type is not public API.
* Bad, because it adds a per-request cache round-trip and CAS-retry cost on
  distributed zones.
* Bad, because correctness depends on a shared clock and a linearizable cache,
  introducing skew/consistency caveats that must be documented and guarded.

### Option B — Literal `governor::state::StateStore` implementation

Implement `governor`'s `StateStore` trait for a cache-backed store, bridging the
synchronous `measure_and_replace` to the async cache with `block_in_place` +
`block_on` (or a synchronous cache client).

* Good, because it maximizes reuse of `governor` (its GCRA and `check_key` are
  used unchanged).
* Good, because the data model still maps cleanly (single arrival-time value).
* Bad, because it blocks a runtime worker for a network round-trip on the hot
  path, harming throughput.
* Bad, because it can panic/deadlock on a current-thread runtime.
* Bad, because the trait's `Fn` closure is designed to be re-run by an in-memory
  CAS loop, which is an awkward fit for a network CAS loop.

### Option C — Local buckets with periodic reconciliation to the cache

Each replica keeps a local bucket and periodically pushes/pulls aggregate counts
through the cache (write-behind / eventual consistency).

* Good, because the hot path stays fully in-process (lowest per-request latency).
* Neutral, because it tolerates weak-consistency caches.
* Bad, because it does not enforce a hard combined limit — bursts up to
  `replica_count ×` the budget are possible within a reconciliation window.
* Bad, because it adds reconciliation and merge-semantics complexity.

### Option D — Do nothing

Keep in-process state and document the caveat.

* Good, because zero cost and zero risk.
* Bad, because it leaves the core problem (per-replica multiplication) unsolved.

## More Information

* Current implementation: `gears/system/api-gateway/src/middleware/throttling.rs`
  (`RateZone`, the `governor` `KeyedRateLimiter`, and `ThrottleKeyPruner`).
* Cluster cache primitive: `ClusterCacheBackend` /
  `ClusterCacheV1` in `gears/system/cluster/cluster-sdk/src/cache/` — versioned
  `CacheEntry { value, version }`, `compare_and_swap`, and per-write TTL.
* Precedent for building coordination on cache CAS, including the linearizability
  guard and weak-consistency opt-in:
  `gears/system/cluster/cluster/src/defaults/lock.rs`
  (`CasBasedDistributedLockBackend`), and cluster DESIGN principle
  `cpt-cf-clst-principle-cas-universal`.
* `governor` `StateStore` contract (synchronous `measure_and_replace`):
  `governor` v0.10.4, `src/state.rs`.

### Not applicable

* **UX / BIZ / COMPL**: no end-user-facing surface, product-requirement, or
  regulatory dimension — this is an internal enforcement mechanism.

## Traceability

This decision annotates the API Gateway throttling design. Related design/spec
elements (to be linked once the gateway DESIGN is updated):

* API Gateway throttling middleware — distributed rate-limit enforcement.
* Cluster cache primitive (`cf-cluster-sdk`) — provides the version-based CAS and
  TTL this decision depends on.
